claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 7 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 7 (under 16)
  - Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child in local authority care for 12 weeks (>8) and does not spend 24+ hours/week at home
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child in local authority care for 12 weeks (>8) and does not spend 24+ hours/week at home
circumstances: Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
Child in local authority care for 12 weeks (>8) and does not spend 24+ hours/week at home
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions.

Let's start with: **Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you inquiring about?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have one child I'm asking about. His name is Alex and he's 7 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! Alex is 7 years old, so he's under 16, which is good for the basic age requirement.

Now I need to ask: **Do you live with Alex in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I live in the UK, but Alex doesn't live with me at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I understand. Alex doesn't currently live with you, but that doesn't automatically mean you can't claim Child Benefit.

**Can you tell me who Alex is living with, and are you making any financial contributions towards his upkeep** (such as paying for clothes, food, pocket money, or contributing to his accommodation)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex is in local authority care right now. But yes, I do contribute towards his upkeep - I help pay for things like his clothes and other essentials.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for sharing that. I understand Alex is in local authority care and you're contributing towards his upkeep.

To help me assess your eligibility properly, I need to know: **How long has Alex been in care? Has it been more than 8 weeks?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: It's been about 3 months now - so yes, that's 12 weeks.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Since Alex has been in care for 12 weeks (3 months), I need to ask an important follow-up question:

**Does Alex spend any regular time at home with you? For example, does he spend at least 24 hours a week at your home, or does he spend 2 nights in a row each week, or 7 days or more in a row at home?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he doesn't come home to stay with me. He's at the care home full-time.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. Let me clarify one more important detail:

**Is the local council paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance costs while he's in care?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I'm not sure about what the council is paying for his placement, but I know I'm contributing £50 a week towards his needs.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that additional information. One final question to complete my assessment:

**Has anyone else claimed Child Benefit for Alex, or is anyone else currently receiving Child Benefit for him?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on the information you've provided, **I'm sorry to inform you that you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex** at this time.

Here's why:

While Alex is 7 years old (which meets the basic age requirement), he has been in local authority care for 12 weeks (3 months), which exceeds the 8-week threshold. Under Child Benefit rules, payments usually stop after a child has been in care for more than 8 weeks.

There is an exception that would allow you to continue claiming if Alex spends **at least 24 hours a week at home with you**, but you've confirmed that Alex stays at the care home full-time and doesn't come home for visits.

Although you're making financial contributions of £50 per week towards his needs—which shows you're supporting him—this alone doesn't meet the requirement for the exception when a child has been in care for more than 8 weeks.

**However, your circumstances could change:** If Alex starts spending regular time at home with you (such as 2 nights in a row each week or 7 days or more in a row), you may be able to claim Child Benefit again. If that happens, you should contact HMRC to discuss your eligibility.

If you have any questions or if your circumstances change, please don't hesitate to reach out.

reasonings: Alex is 7 years old and therefore meets the basic age requirement of being under 16. However, Alex has been in local authority care for 12 weeks (3 months), which exceeds the 8-week threshold. According to the rules, after 8 weeks in care, Child Benefit payments are usually no longer available. There is an exception if the child spends at least 24 hours a week at home, but the claimant has confirmed that Alex does not come home to stay and remains at the care home full-time. While the claimant is making financial contributions of £50 per week towards Alex's upkeep, the exception for continued Child Benefit eligibility after 8 weeks in care requires regular time spent at home (at least 24 hours per week), which is not happening in this case. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex under the current circumstances.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (7 years old), who has been in local authority care for 12 weeks. While the claimant is contributing £50 per week towards Alex's needs, Alex does not spend regular time at home (no 24-hour weekly visits). Under Child Benefit rules, eligibility typically ends after 8 weeks in care unless the child spends at least 24 hours a week at home. Since this exception does not apply, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Under Child Benefit rules, eligibility typically ends after 8 weeks in care unless the child spends at least 24 hours a week at home. Since this exception does not apply, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
